Honest answer: **maybe at an EMNLP workshop, very unlikely at the EMNLP main conference, but it's the right tier to aim for.** Here's the breakdown.

## The realistic venues, ranked

| Venue | Bar | Your odds if 3-way > 2-way |
|---|---|---|
| **EMNLP main** | Strong novelty + SOTA-ish + thorough | Low (~10–15%). Reviewers will say "incremental extension of Jazbec et al." |
| **EMNLP Findings** | Solid methods + clear results, novelty bar lower | Moderate (~25–35%) |
| **EMNLP workshops** (e.g., BlackboxNLP, NLP for Reasoning, Efficient NLP) | Interesting result + clean writeup | **High (~60–80%)** — this is the realistic target |
| **NeurIPS workshops** (e.g., ENLSP, Efficient Generation) | Same | High |
| **ICLR Tiny Papers / blogposts** | Anything well-presented | Very high |
| **arXiv preprint** | Nothing | 100% |

## What "better than 2-way" actually has to look like for each tier

**Workshop bar (achievable):**
- 3-way beats 2-way on ≥1 dataset at the same NFE budget, statistically meaningful (≥3 seeds, error bars not overlapping)
- Honest ablation showing the win comes from the *remasking action* specifically (not just from extra capacity)
- Qualitative analysis showing remasking actually fires on real errors
- Clean writeup, reproducible code

This is realistically what your project will produce. **You can almost certainly get this into a workshop.**

**Findings bar:**
- All of the above, plus: results on multiple datasets (GSM8K + MATH + at least one code benchmark), plus a Pareto frontier showing the win holds across multiple compute budgets, plus a comparison with at least one stronger baseline (RemeDi or LLaDOU)

This is reachable but tight on a course-project timeline.

**Main conference bar:**
- Findings bar + meaningful conceptual novelty beyond "we added an action" + competitive with the best learned method (RemeDi), or some surprising finding that reframes the problem.

This is hard. The reviewer concern will be: "Jazbec et al. already did the hard part — formulating it as an MDP, training the policy with GRPO. You added one action. Why is this a separate paper?"

**Counter-argument you'd need to make:** "The 3rd action requires solving X (e.g., monotonicity constraint, credit assignment for negative actions, exploration of a fundamentally different policy space) which is non-trivial." If your experiments uncover something like "naïve 3-way fails because of X, our trick Y fixes it," that's your novelty story. Without that, it reads as incremental.

## What would actually move the needle

In rough order of effort vs payoff:

1. **Be the first to publish a remasking result without retraining the base model.** RemeDi requires training the 8B base; you don't. *Frame the paper around this efficiency story*, not "we added an action." Title: "Remasking is Free: Learning Error Correction for Frozen Diffusion LLMs."
2. **Show a Pareto frontier where 3-way dominates.** Not "3-way is 1pt higher" — show "at every compute budget, 3-way beats 2-way *and* heuristics, and the gap widens at low NFE."
3. **Find a failure mode of 2-way that 3-way uniquely fixes.** A specific class of errors (e.g., reasoning chain commits early, can't recover) where your qualitative examples are striking.
4. **Negative result is also publishable.** If 3-way *doesn't* clearly beat 2-way on a frozen model, that's a meaningful finding ("you can't cheaply add error correction post-hoc; the base model has to be co-trained, which validates RemeDi's design"). Workshop venues actively want this.

## What I'd actually do

Given you're in week 1 of a 10-week course project, here's the realistic ladder:

1. **Course project deliverable** — that's the actual deadline. Hit it.
2. **arXiv preprint** as soon as the report is solid. Costs nothing, establishes priority, lets you cite it, looks great on a CV.
3. **Workshop submission** — pick the next NLP/ML workshop with a deadline 2–4 weeks after your course ends. Repurpose the report. 80% odds of acceptance if results are clean.
4. **Findings submission** — only if results are unusually strong AND you have a co-author with paper-writing experience to help cross the bar. Otherwise stay at workshop level.

EMNLP main conference: probably not, **but you don't need it to**. A workshop paper from a 10-week course project that becomes a 6-citation arXiv post is a great outcome — and it's an honest path with plays well at every level. Don't let the main-conf bar discourage you from publishing what you'll actually have.

**Bottom line:** Yes, this is publishable somewhere reputable if 3-way > 2-way holds up. Aim for a workshop, frame the story around the "frozen base + free error correction" angle, and treat EMNLP main as a stretch goal that depends on results being unexpectedly strong.
like alpha edit just adds one line of code, and its like the best paper award or smth

True — and that's the right intuition to push back with. AlphaEdit is the perfect counterexample to "incremental = unpublishable." Let me sharpen the actual rule, because it matters for how you frame *your* paper.

## What AlphaEdit actually got right

AlphaEdit (Fang et al., ICLR 2025 Outstanding Paper) is literally one projection matrix added to a prior method (MEMIT). The diff is ~5 lines. It won an outstanding paper award. Why?

1. **Big effect size.** It didn't improve MEMIT by 1%, it fixed sequential editing by ~50% on the key metric. The number was *shocking*, not marginal.
2. **The one line had a principled justification.** Project the parameter update onto the null space of preserved knowledge → editing can't possibly disturb what you wanted to keep. It's a one-line trick with a clean theoretical story behind it.
3. **It diagnosed a problem nobody had named clearly.** "Sequential editing collapses" was a known annoyance; AlphaEdit framed it as the central failure of the paradigm and then solved it.
4. **It worked on multiple models and tasks** without retuning.
5. **Writing was confident and clean.** They didn't apologize for the simplicity — they leaned into it.

The lesson is **not** "small ideas can win." It's: *small ideas with shocking effect sizes, a principled story, and confident framing can win.* Marginal ideas with the same surface area still get rejected.

## How that translates to your project

For "3-way > 2-way" to be your AlphaEdit moment, you'd need:

| AlphaEdit pattern | Your equivalent |
|---|---|
| Big shocking number | 3-way meaningfully beats 2-way *and* the gap widens dramatically at low NFE budgets, e.g. "+8 pts at 16 steps" not "+1 pt at 32 steps" |
| Principled why | A clean explanation: e.g. "the 2-way policy is forced to commit; the 3rd action is the only way to escape early-step entropy collapse" |
| Names a problem | "Frozen-base remasking" as a named, useful capability that didn't exist before |
| Works across settings | Same gains on GSM8K *and* MATH *and* HumanEval, no per-dataset retuning |
| Confident framing | Title, abstract, and intro that don't say "we extend Jazbec et al."; they say "we show that frozen diffusion LLMs can self-correct" |

If your experiments hit those marks, **then yes, EMNLP main is genuinely on the table.** Not because the idea is big, but because the result is surprising and the story is tight.

## The brutal version

What kills "small idea" papers in review isn't the smallness — it's any of:

- Effect size of <2 points where reviewers can argue it's noise
- Only one dataset or only one model
- The trick has tunable hyperparameters that look suspiciously cherry-picked
- The writeup positions it as "extending [prior work]" instead of as a standalone capability
- No theoretical or mechanistic story for *why* the trick works

If you avoid all five of those, the simplicity becomes a *feature* ("look how easy it is!") instead of a liability ("look how trivial it is").

## What this means for your week-1 planning

You don't decide right now whether to aim for EMNLP main. You **set up the experiments so that the AlphaEdit-tier outcome is achievable if the results cooperate.** Concretely:

1. **Always run the Pareto frontier, not just one budget.** Sweep K = 8, 16, 32, 64. If 3-way wins by 1 pt at K=32 but 8 pts at K=8, *that's the headline*. If you only ran K=32 you'd never see it and you'd write a forgettable paper.
2. **Always include the 2-way head-to-head as the central comparison.** Same architecture, same training, same compute — only the action space differs. This is your AlphaEdit ablation: "we changed one thing."
3. **Run on at least 2 datasets from day one** of the real experiments. GSM8K + MATH, or GSM8K + HumanEval. Single-dataset wins get dismissed.
4. **Track *when* remasking fires.** If you can show "remasking activates exactly at the steps where 2-way commits to wrong tokens," that's your mechanistic story.
5. **Have a Plan B framing ready.** If 3-way only barely beats 2-way, the paper becomes "Surprising negative result: post-hoc remasking is bounded by base-model quality, suggesting joint training is necessary." That's still a workshop paper, possibly a Findings paper, and it's honest.

So: **don't lower your ceiling, but don't bet the whole project on hitting it.** Run experiments that let the AlphaEdit-tier story emerge if it's there, and degrade gracefully to a workshop paper if it's not. The same baseline sweep + 2-way-vs-3-way ablation supports both endpoints — you don't have to choose now.

Does that change anything about what you want to launch tonight? The "always run a Pareto frontier" point is the one that might affect the script — you could add `K=8, K=16, K=32, K=64` to the loop instead of just K=32. Costs ~3× more but it's the difference between a forgettable table and a potential headline figure.

Honest assessment:
As a course project / workshop paper: yes, the 3-way policy extension is solid. You have a clear baseline to extend (Jazbec et al.), a clean ablation (2-way vs 3-way), and either outcome is interesting. That's enough for a workshop at NeurIPS or ICML.
For a main NeurIPS/ICML submission: the 3-way extension alone is probably too incremental. You're adding one categorical dimension to someone else's framework. Reviewers would say "neat trick, but is this really a paper?" You'd need a stronger story.
To make it main-conference worthy, you'd need one of these escalations:
Option A: The LoRA bridge. Train the policy + LoRA on the frozen LLaDA jointly. This positions the paper as "middle ground between fully frozen external policy (Jazbec) and fully retrained dual-stream (RemeDi)." You'd have a clean 3-point comparison: frozen policy < frozen policy + LoRA < RemeDi, with the middle option being 100x cheaper than RemeDi. The contribution becomes about the training paradigm, not just the action space.
Option B: Deeper analysis of when remasking matters. Don't just show it helps — show why and when. Characterize the types of errors that remasking can fix with a frozen model vs. errors that require distribution changes. This turns the paper from "we tried a thing" into "we provide understanding of error correction in diffusion LLMs." If remasking doesn't help much in the frozen setting, that's actually a strong finding — it proves that the value of RemeDi's dual-stream comes from joint distribution improvement, not just the remasking mechanism.
Option C: Generalize beyond LLaDA. Show the policy transfers across base models (LLaDA → Dream), across domains (math → code → general text), and across sequence lengths. If a single tiny policy works plug-and-play across models, that's a much bigger story than one model on one task.
My honest recommendation: scope the course project as the 3-way extension (workshop-level). If the results are promising, spend the summer pushing Option A or B for a main conference submission. Don't try to do everything at once — the 3-way ablation alone will tell you which direction to escalate.

Honest ranking, accounting for what you actually have (10 weeks, $300 + VESSL maybe, 3 people, L4 + Spot A100):

## TL;DR ranking

1. **Option B** (deeper analysis) — most realistic, highest paper-value-per-hour
2. **Option A** (LoRA bridge) — realistic but compute-risky
3. **Option C** (generalize across models) — least realistic on your timeline

Here's why.

## Option C: cut it

Two base models × multiple domains × multiple lengths = ~6× the experiment matrix you already can't fully afford. Dream-7B alone doubles the eval cost and the engineering surface (different tokenizer, different masking convention, different denoising loop in upstream). And the story "it transfers" only lands if it *does* transfer — if it doesn't, you've burned 4 weeks for a negative result you can't even frame well.

The only world where C is worth it: you've already nailed B, have 3 weeks left, and one teammate has nothing to do. Don't plan around it.

## Option A: realistic but risky

LoRA + policy joint training is conceptually clean and the framing is genuinely strong ("middle ground at 100× less cost than RemeDi"). The reviewer story writes itself.

**Why it's risky:**
- LoRA on an 8B base + GRPO rollouts is more compute than pure policy training. Your existing ~4–5 day/epoch estimate roughly doubles.
- More moving parts → more debugging time. Joint optimization of LoRA + tiny policy is finicky; learning rates, KL constraints, and warm-up all need tuning.
- If LoRA training is unstable (very plausible in week 6), you have nothing to fall back on for the report.
- Spot preemption hurts more here — LoRA optimizer state is bigger than the policy alone, and losing 100 steps of joint training matters more than losing 100 steps of pure-policy training.

**When to pick A:** if your 3-way result is *clearly positive but underwhelming* (say, +1.5 pts on GSM8K). The LoRA bridge then becomes your "and here's how to push it further" escalation. But you commit to A only after you know.

## Option B: this is the one

Why B is the realistic best bet:

1. **It uses the experiments you're already running.** The full baseline sweep + 2-way-vs-3-way ablation + Pareto frontier are already in the plan. Option B is mostly a *writing and analysis* layer on top of experiments you must run anyway.
2. **It's robust to either outcome.** If 3-way wins big → "We characterize the regime where post-hoc remasking suffices, and identify when joint training (RemeDi) is actually necessary." If 3-way wins small → "We provide evidence that remasking value primarily comes from base-model distribution improvement, not the remasking mechanism — a finding that justifies RemeDi's design choice." **Both outcomes are publishable, both are interesting, and the analysis pipeline is the same.**
3. **It costs almost nothing extra in compute.** The marginal experiments are small: a remasking-frequency tracker (one logging hook), error-class taxonomy (annotate 100 GSM8K errors by hand or with GPT-4 as a judge), and a few qualitative trajectories. Maybe ~$20 more in eval cost.
4. **The reviewer story is specifically what main-conference reviewers reward.** "Understanding when X works" papers age better than "X works" papers. Reviewers love characterization papers because they're easier to extend.
5. **It complements rather than competes with future work.** A cleanly-characterized B paper makes a follow-up A paper much easier to write — you'd already have the failure-mode analysis showing exactly why LoRA helps.

**The concrete experiments B adds, in priority order:**

1. **Remasking-frequency-by-step plot.** Log how often remasking fires at each denoising step. Does it spike early? Late? Cluster around specific token positions? This is one logging hook + one matplotlib call.
2. **Error taxonomy.** On a sample of GSM8K errors, classify: "remasking fixed it," "remasking would have to fix it but didn't," "remasking can't help — base model is wrong about the answer." This gives you the headline finding: *what fraction of errors are correctable by remasking alone?*
3. **Pareto frontier sweep.** K = 8, 16, 32, 64. As discussed earlier — single most likely path to a "shocking number."
4. **Qualitative trajectories.** 4–6 side-by-side examples in the report appendix where 2-way commits to a wrong token and 3-way recovers. Reviewer eye-candy.
5. **One sentence ablation:** turn off remasking at inference time on the trained 3-way policy. If accuracy drops a lot → remasking is doing real work. If it doesn't drop → policy learned to ignore remasking, which is *also* a finding.

None of these require new infrastructure. Items 1, 4, 5 are essentially free. Item 2 takes a few hours of human labeling. Item 3 you'd run anyway.

## My actual recommendation for your project

- **Now → CP1**: 2-way reproduction, baseline sweep. (Already planned.)
- **CP1 → CP2**: launch full GSM8K Pareto frontier for heuristics and 2-way. Add the remasking-frequency logging hook. Write Intro + Related Work + Baseline Setup using draft language from cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md.
- **CP2 → mid-project**: train 3-way, run head-to-head with 2-way on Pareto frontier. **Decide here**: if results are clear-positive, lean into B's analysis layer for the report. If results are flat, pivot to "characterization paper" framing — still B, but with the negative-result story.
- **Mid-project → final**: add error taxonomy, qualitative examples, ablate remasking-off-at-inference. Polish writing.
- **Course report → summer**: *only then* consider A as a follow-up paper.

This sequence gives you:
- Workshop paper guaranteed, no matter what 3-way looks like
- Realistic shot at Findings if numbers cooperate
- Outside shot at main conference if you nail the characterization angle
- A clean follow-up project (Option A) for summer if you want to keep going

**Don't pick A or C now.** Pick B because it's a writing/analysis upgrade on the experiments you're already committed to, and it leaves both A and C as future options. The worst case is "we ran the planned experiments and wrote a thoughtful analysis paper." That's already a workshop submission.

what is detach from tmux? you mean exit ssh?

No — they're different things. Quick mental model:

- **tmux session** = a persistent terminal living *on the VM*. Survives SSH disconnects. Your `./run_all_baselines.sh` runs inside it.
- **SSH connection** = your laptop's pipe into the VM. Closing it doesn't kill anything inside tmux.

**Detach** = step out of the tmux session but leave it (and your script) running. Keystroke: **Ctrl-B**, release, then **D**. You'll drop back to a normal shell prompt on the VM, and you'll see something like `[detached (from session baselines)]`. The script keeps running.

After detaching you can:
- Just close the SSH window — script keeps going
- Or run other commands on the VM (`nvidia-smi`, `tail -f logs_baseline-random-K32.txt`, etc.)

**Reattach** later (from a new SSH session) with:
```bash
tmux attach -t baselines
```

You'll be back inside the running session, see live progress, and can detach again.

**Don't confuse with `exit`**: typing `exit` inside tmux closes the tmux session and **kills your script**. Always use Ctrl-B D to leave.

So the order is: launch script → Ctrl-B D → close SSH if you want → come back later → SSH in → `tmux attach -t baselines`.
what is tmux?

**tmux** = "terminal multiplexer." It's a program that runs on the VM and gives you a terminal session that doesn't die when you disconnect.

## The problem it solves

Without tmux:
1. You SSH into the L4
2. Start `./run_all_baselines.sh` (12+ hours)
3. Your laptop sleeps / Wi-Fi blips / you close the lid
4. SSH disconnects → **the script gets killed mid-run** → all progress lost

This is the default Unix behavior: when your shell dies, every program it started dies with it.

## How tmux fixes it

tmux runs as a separate background process on the VM. Anything started inside tmux is owned by tmux, not by your SSH connection. So:

1. SSH into the L4
2. `tmux new -s baselines` ← creates a tmux session named "baselines"
3. Start `./run_all_baselines.sh` *inside* the tmux session
4. **Ctrl-B then D** → "detach" — you leave the tmux session but it keeps running
5. Close your laptop, go to sleep, whatever
6. Tomorrow: SSH back in → `tmux attach -t baselines` → you're back inside, watching live progress

The script doesn't know or care that you came and went. It just sees a stable terminal.

## Other things tmux can do (you don't need them today)

- Multiple "windows" inside one session (like browser tabs)
- Split panes (two terminals side by side)
- Share a session with another user

For your case, only two commands matter:

```bash
tmux new -s baselines           # start a session called "baselines"
tmux attach -t baselines        # come back to it later
```

And one keystroke: **Ctrl-B then D** to detach.

That's it. Think of it as "screen recording for your terminal that you can rewind into."

(base) ijinyu1113@dllm-l4:~$ sudo apt install -y tmux   # if not already
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
Reading state information... Done
tmux is already the newest version (3.1c-1+deb11u1).
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 17 not upgraded.
(base) ijinyu1113@dllm-l4:~$ tmux new -s baselines
[detached (from session baselines)]
(base) ijinyu1113@dllm-l4:~$ 
so this is running? i keep ssh alive?

No — you accidentally **detached immediately** without starting the script. The tmux session `baselines` exists and is empty (just sitting at a shell prompt doing nothing). Your script is *not* running yet.

The most likely cause: you hit Ctrl-B D out of habit, or your terminal forwarded a keystroke that detached you.

## Reattach and start the script

```bash
tmux attach -t baselines
```

You'll be back inside the tmux session. Now actually run the script:

```bash
cd ~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM
conda activate dllm
./run_all_baselines.sh
```

You should immediately see output like `=== Starting baseline-random-K32 ===` and then model loading / progress bars. **Wait until you see real progress before detaching.** Confirms it's actually running.

Then detach: **Ctrl-B**, release both keys, then press **D**. You'll see `[detached (from session baselines)]` and drop back to the plain shell prompt. The script keeps running.

## After detaching

- You can close SSH safely — script keeps going
- Or keep SSH alive and check progress with: `tmux attach -t baselines` (then Ctrl-B D again to leave)
- Tail the log without attaching: `tail -f ~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/logs_baseline-random-K32.txt`

## How to verify it's actually running right now

After detaching, run:
```bash
tmux ls
```

You should see something like:
```
baselines: 1 windows (created Tue Apr  8 ...)
```

And:
```bash
nvidia-smi
```

Should show ~16–20 GB of GPU memory in use and a python process. If GPU memory is 0, the script isn't running.
i did start the script. i saw some checkpoints

Good — that means tmux *is* doing its job. You started the script, saw the checkpoint loading, and then detached (intentionally or by accident). The script is still running on the VM.

## Verify it's actually running

```bash
tmux ls
```

Should show `baselines: 1 windows ...`.

```bash
nvidia-smi
```

Should show ~16–20 GB GPU memory used and a `python` process. **This is the most reliable check** — if GPU is busy, the script is running.

```bash
tail -f ~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/logs_baseline-random-K32.txt
```

Live log tail. Ctrl-C to stop tailing (does *not* kill the script).

## Now you can safely:

- Close the SSH window — script keeps running
- Close your laptop — script keeps running
- Come back tomorrow → SSH in → `tmux attach -t baselines` to see live progress, or `tmux ls` + `tail -f logs_*.txt` to peek without attaching

## Reminder about cost

The L4 is now billing at ~$0.88/hr for the next ~12–15 hours = ~$11–14. Set the **$50 budget alert** (Billing → Budgets & alerts) if you haven't, as a safety net.

When all three baselines finish (look for `ALL BASELINES DONE` in the log or `tmux attach`), **stop the VM immediately**:

```bash
gcloud compute instances stop dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a
```

Or browser console → VM instances → check `dllm-l4` → STOP.

Perfect — it's running exactly as it should:

- ✅ **GPU-Util: 99%** — fully working, not idle
- ✅ **Memory: 16 GB / 23 GB** — LLaDA-8B in bf16 loaded
- ✅ **Power: 71/72 W** — pegged at the L4's power limit (good, you're getting full throughput)
- ✅ **Process: python3.12 in dllm env** — your script
- ✅ tmux session alive

**You're done for now.** Close the SSH window, close your laptop, walk away. Come back in ~12–15 hours.

## When you come back

```bash
# from your laptop
gcloud compute ssh dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a

# on the VM
tmux attach -t baselines
```

Look for `ALL BASELINES DONE` in the log. If you see it → **stop the VM immediately**:

```bash
exit                                                       # detach: Ctrl-B D first if inside tmux
gcloud compute instances stop dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a
```

If it's still running, just detach (Ctrl-B D) and check again later.

Sleep well.
do i need to open the same vm?

Yes — same VM. The script, the model weights, the partial results, the tmux session, all of it lives on `dllm-l4`'s disk. A different VM would have none of that.

```bash
gcloud compute ssh dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a
```

If the VM is **stopped**, start it first (browser console → VM instances → check `dllm-l4` → START, or `gcloud compute instances start dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a`). But you didn't stop it before walking away — it's still running and billing — so just SSH straight in.

Once in:
```bash
tmux attach -t baselines
```
i keep vm running right?

Yes — the VM has to stay running for the script to keep going. Stopping it kills the script.

The cost is the tradeoff: ~$0.88/hr × ~12–15 hours = ~$11–14 total for the whole baseline sweep. That's the price of letting it finish unattended.

**Stop it the moment the script finishes** (`ALL BASELINES DONE` in the log). Every hour you forget afterward is another $0.88 wasted on an idle GPU.
how long will it take?

| Baseline | Problems (gsm8k+math+humaneval+mbpp) | Wall time |
|---|---|---|
| random-K32 | ~2,483 | ~4 hr |
| low_confidence-K32 | ~2,483 | ~4 hr |
| fastdllm-t0.7 | ~2,483 | ~4 hr (variable — threshold can be faster or slower depending on how many tokens cross 0.7 per step) |
| **Total** | ~7,450 | **~12–15 hr** |

So if you launched around 5 AM, expect it to finish sometime between **5 PM and 8 PM today**.

Quick ways to check progress without disturbing it:

```bash

```

Look for the `it/s` rate in the tqdm bar — if it's tracking ~5 sec/it, on schedule. If much slower, something's degraded and worth investigating.

input.content:
# Handoff — Person A baseline run

Short doc to pick up where I left off. For background read [README.md](README.md), [INFRA.md](INFRA.md), [cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md](cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md).

## What's running right now

A full heuristic-baseline sweep is running on the GCP L4 VM, started ~5 AM 2026-04-09.

- **VM**: `dllm-l4` in zone `us-central1-a` (GCP project `cs288-remasking`)
- **Tmux session**: `baselines`
- **Script**: `~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/run_all_baselines.sh`
- **Sweep**: 3 baselines × 4 datasets × 1 seed
  - `baseline-random-K32`
  - `baseline-low_confidence-K32`
  - `baseline-fastdllm-t0.7`
  - Datasets: gsm8k, math, humaneval, mbpp
  - Seed: 42 only (cheap pass for CP2; more seeds later)
- **Expected wall time**: ~12–15 hr from start → finish between ~5 PM and 8 PM 2026-04-09
- **Expected cost**: ~$11–14 of GCP credits at L4 = $0.88/hr
- **GRPO training is NOT running** — that's deferred to Spot A100 later

## Reconnecting from a new device

```bash
# 1. SSH (need gcloud installed + `gcloud auth login` once on the new device)
gcloud compute ssh dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a

# 2. If VM was stopped, start it first:
gcloud compute instances start dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a

# 3. Re-enter the tmux session
tmux attach -t baselines
# detach again with: Ctrl-B then D
```

## Checking progress without attaching tmux

```bash
# Is the GPU busy?  Should show ~16 GB used, ~99% util, python3.12 process.
nvidia-smi

# Is the tmux session still alive?
tmux ls

# Live tail the current baseline's log
ls -lt ~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/logs_*.txt   # newest = currently running
tail -f ~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/logs_baseline-random-K32.txt
# Ctrl-C to stop tailing — does NOT kill the script
```

## Is it done?

Look for the literal string `ALL BASELINES DONE` in the most recent log, or in the tmux session output. If you see it, the script finished cleanly.

If it died partway:
- Check the log for the error
- Each baseline writes to its own `eval_results/<NAME>/` directory, so completed baselines are not lost
- You can re-run only the failed one by editing the `for NAME in ...` loop in `run_all_baselines.sh`

## STOP THE VM as soon as the script finishes

This is the single most important post-run step. Idle L4 = $0.88/hr wasted.

```bash
# from inside tmux: Ctrl-B then D to detach first
exit                                                       # leave SSH
gcloud compute instances stop dllm-l4 --zone us-central1-a
```

Or browser console → Compute Engine → VM instances → check `dllm-l4` → STOP.

Stopped ≠ deleted. Files persist. You can start it again any time.

## Where the results live

```
~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/eval_results/
├── baseline-random-K32/
│   ├── baseline-random-K32/
│   │   └── checkpoint-baseline-random-K32_seed_42_temp_policy_0.0/
│   │       └── <dataset>_..._generations.json
│   ├── detailed_results.csv          ← per-run metrics
│   ├── summary_statistics.csv        ← aggregated by config
│   └── results_report.txt            ← human-readable summary table
├── baseline-low_confidence-K32/
│   └── (same structure)
└── baseline-fastdllm-t0.7/
    └── (same structure)
```

The aggregator runs automatically at the end of each baseline. If for some reason it didn't, run it manually:

```bash
python -m eval.aggregate_results --results_dir eval_results/baseline-random-K32
```

## Interpreting the results for CP2

What you want from each baseline, per dataset:

| Metric | Where to find it | What it means |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | `summary_statistics.csv` column `accuracy` | Exact-match (gsm8k/math) or pass@1 (humaneval/mbpp) |
| **NFEs** | `summary_statistics.csv` column `avg_nfes` | Number of LLaDA forward passes per problem. ~32 for K32 baselines. Critical x-axis for the Pareto frontier. |
| **Wall time** | `summary_statistics.csv` column `avg_wall_time` | Sec per problem. Sanity check; not for the paper. |

For the **CP2 baseline table**, fill in this layout per dataset:

| Method | NFE | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| Random (K=32) | 32 | _from results_report.txt_ |
| Top-K confidence (K=32) | 32 | _from results_report.txt_ |
| Fast-dLLM (t=0.7) | _from results_report.txt_ | _from results_report.txt_ |
| Learned 2-way (Jazbec et al.) | 32 | **TBD — training in progress** |

### Sanity-check the numbers

Compare against the **published numbers** from Jazbec et al. (arXiv:2512.09106). On GSM8K with LLaDA-8B-Instruct, K=32, expect roughly:

- Random: ~30–40%
- Top-K confidence (low_confidence): ~60–65%
- Fast-dLLM (t=0.7): ~55–60% (depends heavily on threshold)

If your numbers are in those ballparks → trust them, write them up. If they're wildly off (e.g., GSM8K top-K at 5% or 95%), something's wrong with the eval setup — flag it before writing the report.

## Pulling results to your local machine

```bash
# from your laptop, NOT the VM
gcloud compute scp --recurse \
  dllm-l4:~/Learned-Remasking-Policy-for-dLLM/eval_results \
  ./eval_results \
  --zone us-central1-a
```

Then commit them to the repo:

```bash
git add eval_results/
git commit -m "Person A: heuristic baseline results (seed 42, all 4 datasets)"
git push
```

## What's next after baselines land

1. **Write CP2 sections** using the table above + content from [cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md](cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md):
   - Intro (lift from §2 + §9)
   - Related Work (lift from §11, expand each bullet)
   - Baseline Setup (this run + INFRA.md content)
   - VESSL credits paragraph (template in earlier conversation)
2. **Person B** starts on the 3-way action head — see [INFRA.md §"Where Person B plugs in the 3-way action"](INFRA.md).
3. **Person C** starts evaluation harness for the 3-way policy once it's training.
4. Eventually: spin up the **Spot A100** (separate VM, see [INFRA.md](INFRA.md)) for GRPO training. L4 stays for eval.

## Common pitfalls

- **`exit` inside tmux kills the script.** Always use Ctrl-B D to leave tmux.
- **Don't forget to stop the VM.** $0.88/hr × 1 night of forgetting = ~$8 wasted. Set a $50 budget alert in GCP Billing as a safety net.
- **MBPP and HumanEval may fail** if the VM doesn't have a working `python` for code execution. If logs show `mbpp` or `humaneval` errors, paste the traceback into a new Claude session — there's a known fix that involves installing `python3` as a system binary or adjusting the eval harness.
- **The aggregator sometimes silently misses files** if the directory layout doesn't match its glob. If `summary_statistics.csv` is empty or missing, run `python -m eval.aggregate_results --results_dir eval_results/<NAME>` manually.

#
# For licensing see accompanying LICENSE file.

#
### Adapted from https://github.com/dllm-reasoning/d1 (Apache 2.0)
# ModelConfig
torch_dtype: bfloat16
load_in_4bit: false
model_path: GSAI-ML/LLaDA-8B-Instruct

attn_implementation: sdpa

# GRPOConfig
dataset: gsm8k_and_math
seed: 123
bf16: false

adam_beta1: 0.9
adam_beta2: 0.99
weight_decay: 0.1
max_grad_norm: 0.2

use_vllm: false
vllm_device: auto
vllm_gpu_memory_utilization: 0.9

warmup_steps: 100
logging_steps: 1
learning_rate: 3e-5
lr_scheduler_type: cosine
reward_functions:
  - mixed_correctness_mult_reward_func
  - mixed_correctness_reward_func
reward_weights:
  - 1.0
  - 0.0

num_generations: 8
num_train_epochs: 1

per_device_eval_batch_size: 1
per_device_train_batch_size: 16
gradient_accumulation_steps: 1

gradient_checkpointing: false
gradient_checkpointing_kwargs:
  use_reentrant: false

resume_from_checkpoint: true

# Parameters for saving checkpoints
save_steps: 50
save_strategy: steps
save_total_limit: 500

# Diffusion and GRPO-specific
max_completion_length: 256
max_prompt_length: 200
block_length: 32
generation_batch_size: 16
remasking: policy
beta: 0.
epsilon: 0.5
num_iterations: 2
loglikelihood_dtype: float32

# Policy specific
policy_type: dit_confidence
policy_hidden_dim: 128
policy_feedforward_dim: 512
policy_num_heads: 2
policy_num_blocks: 1
policy_dropout: 0.0
policy_time_embed_dim: 128
policy_full_context: true
policy_smart_init: -2.0

content.text:
Confirmed. Your current baseline config:

| Parameter | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **`alpha_compute_reward`** | **`0.0`** (default — not in YAML) | **Pure accuracy, no efficiency penalty.** R = correctness only; NFE doesn't affect the reward. |
| `block_length` | 32 | Semi-AR with 32-token blocks |
| `remasking` | `policy` (but baselines override this via the checkpoint name) | Heuristic baselines ignore this |
| `reward_functions` | `mixed_correctness_mult_reward_func` (weight 1.0) | Multiplicative correctness reward |
| `num_generations` | 8 | G=8 trajectories per prompt for GRPO |
| `policy_smart_init` | -2.0 | Bias toward keeping tokens masked early in training |

**For the heuristic baselines you're running right now, alpha doesn't matter** — there's no policy being optimized, so the reward function is never called. Alpha only matters when you train the 2-way or 3-way policy with GRPO later.

Per [plan §3.1](cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md), you'll eventually sweep α ∈ {0, 0.3, 1, 3, 10} to trace the accuracy-vs-efficiency Pareto frontier. I also see you already have 3-way configs with `alpha_compute_reward: 0.0` and `1.0` ready in the repo.

content.text:
You have two 3-way configs. Which one are you running? Do you know which config you passed to `train.train`?

- **`BL32_3way_alpha0.yaml`** → `alpha_compute_reward: 0.0` — **pure accuracy, no NFE penalty**
- **`BL32_3way_alpha1.yaml`** → `alpha_compute_reward: 1.0` — **accuracy + efficiency penalty (penalizes more forward passes)**

If you're not sure, check on the machine that's training:

```bash
# look at the command that's running
ps aux | grep train.train
```

Or check if there's a wandb/log file that records the config. The config filename in the command will tell you which alpha you're using.

This is the right one to start with. Train for accuracy first, prove 3-way > 2-way on correctness alone. The α=1.0 sweep (accuracy + NFE penalty) comes later for the Pareto frontier.

Let me compare the two runs carefully.

Run 1: `3way_alpha0_4090` (Ryan's 4090)
- GPU: RTX 4090
- per_device_train_batch_size: 4
- num_generations: 4
- generation_batch_size: 4
- global_step: 290
- Runtime: 50m 30s

Run 2: `3way_alpha0_bl32_mixture_v2` (Ijin's A100 on GCP)
- GPU: A100-SXM4-40GB


- per_device_train_batch_size: 16
- num_generations: 8
- generation_batch_size: 16
- global_step: 1,098
- Runtime: 19h 21m

The A100 run used much larger batch sizes and more generations per step, reaching nearly 4x the training steps in significantly more wall-clock time. But looking at the actual training metrics, both runs are showing concerning results.

The real problem is that reward, loss, and gradient norms are all zero across both experiments. Since GRPO computes advantages relative to each group's mean reward, when every completion in a group receives identical rewards, all advantages collapse to zero—which means zero loss, zero gradients, and effectively no learning happening at all. The policy is completely stuck, with nearly all tokens staying masked and almost no meaningful action diversity.

Looking at the differences between runs, Ryan's setup uses explicit batch size and generation parameters (batch size 4, 4 generations), while Ijin's relies on config defaults. The denoising step counts also differ notably—Ryan's at 146 versus Ijin's at 256—which might relate to block length or batch configuration, though I need to check what's actually driving that variation.

But the real problem overshadows any config differences: both runs are completely broken with zero reward across all completions, meaning there's no learning signal at all. The masking behavior and fixed step counts suggest something fundamental is wrong with the training setup itself, not just the parameter choices.

content.text:
Two things to address: the config difference, and a **much bigger problem** both runs share.

## Config difference

Ryan's 4090 run overrides three params via CLI that your A100 run uses defaults for:

| Parameter | Ryan (4090) | You (A100) | Default in YAML |
|---|---|---|---|
| `per_device_train_batch_size` | **4** | 16 | 16 |
| `num_generations` | **4** | 8 | 8 |
| `generation_batch_size` | **4** | 16 | 16 |

His command has `--per_device_train_batch_size 4 --num_generations 4 --generation_batch_size 4` — the 4090 has 24 GB vs your A100's 40 GB, so he shrunk everything to fit. This changes:
- **G=4 vs G=8** for GRPO group size (fewer trajectories per prompt → noisier advantage estimates)
- **Effective batch size** is 4× smaller on his run
- His `num_steps_mean=146` vs yours `num_steps_mean=256` — likely a downstream effect of different batch/generation sizing

## The real problem: both runs are completely broken

Look at the metrics both runs share:

```
train/reward:          0
train/reward_std:      0
train/zero_std_ratio:  1        ← every single group has zero variance
train/loss:            0
train/grad_norm:       0
train/entropy:         ~0.07–0.15  ← collapsing toward determinism
train/action_rate/keep:    ~97–98%
train/action_rate/unmask:  ~1–3%
train/action_rate/remask:  ~0.00005–0.00001%
```

This means:
1. **Reward = 0 for every completion.** The model never produces a correct answer. Not once across 290 or 1098 steps.
2. **zero_std_ratio = 1** → in every GRPO group, all G completions get the same reward (0). GRPO computes advantages as reward minus group mean — if all rewards are identical, all advantages are zero.
3. **Zero advantages → zero loss → zero gradients → no learning.** The policy is frozen in place, not updating at all.
4. **The policy is almost never unmasking.** `action_rate/keep ≈ 98%` means the policy keeps nearly everything masked. Only ~2% of tokens get unmasked per step. After 32 denoising steps, most tokens are still `[MASK]` → the output is garbage → reward = 0. A vicious cycle.
5. **Entropy collapsing** — the policy is becoming more and more deterministic (toward always predicting "keep"), not exploring.

**Both runs have the exact same fundamental failure, regardless of batch size.** The config difference is cosmetic — neither run is learning anything.

## Why this is happening

The `policy_smart_init: -2.0` biases the policy toward keeping tokens masked at the start of training (this is intentional — start conservative, let GRPO push toward more unmasking). But with 3-way actions, the initialization may be too conservative: the policy is so confident in "keep" that it **never unmasks enough tokens to produce a readable answer**, so reward is always 0, so there's no gradient signal to push it toward unmasking more. It's a **cold start / exploration failure**.

This is the risk flagged in [plan §8](cs288_remasking_policy_plan.md) row 1: "Remasking rarely fires (policy learns to never remask)." Except it's worse — the policy isn't even *unmasking*.

## Likely fixes (in order of try-this-first)

1. **Raise `policy_smart_init` from -2.0 to something like -0.5 or 0.0.** This makes the initial policy less biased toward "keep," so it unmasks more aggressively at the start of training. The 2-way baseline uses -2.0 because with only 2 actions the sigmoid still gives reasonable unmask rates — but with 3-way softmax, -2.0 on the "keep" logit crushes the other two actions.

2. **Initialize the unmask logit higher than keep/remask.** Instead of uniform initialization with a bias, explicitly set the output layer bias to something like `[+1.0, -2.0, -3.0]` for `[unmask, keep, remask]` so the policy starts by aggressively unmasking (like the heuristic baselines), then learns when to hold back or remask.

3. **Warm-start from the 2-way checkpoint.** Train the 2-way policy first (which works — Jazbec et al. proved it), then initialize the 3-way policy's unmask/keep weights from the 2-way checkpoint and add the remask head with a negative bias. This guarantees the policy starts in a regime where it can already produce correct answers.

4. **Add an entropy bonus** to the reward to prevent the policy from collapsing to deterministic "keep." Something like `reward += 0.01 * entropy`.

**I'd try fix 1 first** — it's one line in the YAML and directly addresses the cold-start problem. If `policy_smart_init: 0.0` still gives reward=0, move to fix 2.

Want me to look at where `policy_smart_init` is used in the code so we can understand exactly how it affects the 3-way action distribution?

content.thinking:
The 2-way run also shows reward=0, zero_std_ratio=1, loss=0, grad_norm=0. So the 2-way baseline is ALSO not learning. This is the same fundamental problem across all three runs.

But wait — this is the original Jazbec et al. config (`llada_8b_instruct_dit_confidence_BL32_mixture.yaml`), the 2-way policy. And it's also getting reward=0 everywhere. This means the problem isn't 3-way specific — it's something more fundamental.


Looking at the 2-way metrics, the unmask probability is around 12.4% per position, entropy is 0.36, and it's taking roughly 193 denoising steps on average. The model is almost fully unmasking tokens by the end (only 0.3% still masked), but the reward signal remains stuck at zero across the board.

This is puzzling — if the policy is generating nearly complete sequences, some of them should be correct by chance alone, especially given that LLaDA-8B achieves 63% on GSM8K with basic heuristics. The fact that zero out of ~128,000 completions are scoring any reward points suggests the problem might not be insufficient unmasking, but rather something wrong with how the reward is being calculated or assigned.

Looking at the 2-way run more closely, the `num_steps_mean: 192.8` is suspiciously high. With a block length of 32 and max completion length of 256, I need to think through how many denoising steps that actually translates to — it seems like the model might be taking far more steps than expected, which could indicate something's off with how the denoising process is structured.

But the real issue is that both the 2-way and 3-way runs are getting reward=0 across the board, even though the 2-way baseline should theoretically work based on the Jazbec et al. paper. This points to either a bug in the reward function itself (maybe it's too strict with answer parsing), the model generating invalid output despite tokens being placed, or a configuration issue that's affecting both runs. The user mentioned wanting to match the 2-way config from a previous run that was done by a teammate on Modal with an A100-80GB GPU. I need to check the actual configs used in both runs since the wandb logs don't show generation_batch_size directly. The 2-way run used the default upstream config with `generation_batch_size: 128`, and the 3-way run also didn't override batch sizes, so it's pulling from its own config file — but I can't see what value that actually has without checking the file itself. So they should match now. The real issue here is that all the runs are returning reward=0, which is the critical problem I need to flag—the config alignment is secondary to debugging why the reward signal is completely absent.

content.text:
The 2-way run has the **exact same problem**: `reward: 0`, `zero_std_ratio: 1`, `loss: 0`, `grad_norm: 0`. All three runs — your 3-way on A100, Ryan's 3-way on 4090, and londine's 2-way on A100-80GB — are completely failing to learn.

This means **it's not a 3-way vs 2-way issue. Something more fundamental is broken.**

## What's suspicious

The 2-way run tells the story clearly:

```
still_masked:          0.003     ← almost all tokens ARE being unmasked
mean_unmask_prob:      0.124     ← 12.4% unmask rate per step — reasonable
num_steps_mean:        192       ← lots of denoising steps happening
completion_length:     239       ← nearly full 256-token completions
reward:                0         ← but ZERO completions are ever "correct"
zero_std_ratio:        1         ← across ALL groups, every completion scores 0
```

The model is generating full sequences. They're just **never scoring as correct**. Across 1004 steps × 16 batch × 8 generations = ~128,000 completions with reward = 0 is extremely suspicious. LLaDA-8B-Instruct gets ~63% on GSM8K with basic heuristic baselines — it should stumble onto *some* correct answers even with a bad policy.

## Most likely cause: the reward function's answer parser is failing

`mixed_correctness_mult_reward_func` has to parse the model's output (extract the boxed answer, compare to ground truth). If the parser can't find the answer in the expected format, it returns 0. Let me check.

68	    run_name=None,
69	    pos_reward=1.0,
70	    alpha=1.0,
71	    **kwargs,
72	) -> list[float]:
73	    r"""Extracts answers from the completions using `parsing_fn`, then computes a reward
74	    of the form $\delta(\hat{y} = y) \cdot \left(\frac{L - steps + 1}{L}\right)^\alpha.
75	    Uses float comparison of extracted numbers instead of exact string matching.
76	    """
77	
78	    responses = [completion[0]["content"] for completion in completions]
79	
80	    if kwargs["dataset_type"][0] == "kodcode":
81	        parsed_responses = []
82	        for i, r in enumerate(responses):
83	            raw_prompt = kwargs["raw_prompt"][i]
84	            if not raw_prompt.endswith("\n"):
85	                raw_prompt = raw_prompt + "\n"
86	            parsed_responses.append(
87	                parsing_fn(raw_prompt + r, kwargs["function_name"][i])
88	            )
89	
90	    else:
91	        parsed_responses = [parsing_fn(r) for r in responses]
92	
93	    correctness_rewards = process_answers_fn(parsed_responses, answer, pos_reward)
94	
95	    # Apply step-based scaling based on alpha:
96	    # - Alpha = 0.0: no scaling
97	    # - 0 < Alpha < 1: Gentle falloff (e.g., α=0.5 is square root)
98	    # - Alpha = 1: Linear decay
99	    # - Alpha > 1: Steep falloff (e.g., α=2 is quadratic)
100	    # - At step L: Goes to (1/L)^α, not exactly 0
101	    # NOTE: Clamps steps to max at L to make the math simpler.
102	    if alpha == 0.0:
103	        return correctness_rewards
104	    else:
105	        return [
106	            reward * ((L - min(steps.item(), L) + 1) / L) ** alpha
107	            for reward, steps in zip(correctness_rewards, n_steps)
108	        ]
109	
110	
111	def _additive_compute_reward_func(
112	    parsing_fn: Callable[[str], float | str | None],
113	    process_answers_fn: Callable[[list[float], list[float], float], list[float]],
114	    prompts,
115	    completions,
116	    answer,
117	    n_steps: torch.Tensor,
118	    L: int,
119	    step=None,
120	    run_name=None,
121	    pos_reward=1.0,
122	    alpha=1.0,
123	    **kwargs,
124	) -> list[float]:
125	    r"""Extracts answers from the completions using `parsing_fn`, then computes a reward
126	    of the form $\delta(\hat{y} = y) \cdot \left(\frac{L - steps + 1}{L}\right)^\alpha.
127	    Uses float comparison of extracted numbers instead of exact string matching.
128	    """
129	    responses = [completion[0]["content"] for completion in completions]
130	
131	    if kwargs.get("dataset_type", [None])[0] == "kodcode":
132	        parsed_responses = []
133	        for i, r in enumerate(responses):
134	            raw_prompt = kwargs["raw_prompt"][i]
135	            if not raw_prompt.endswith("\n"):
136	                raw_prompt = raw_prompt + "\n"
137	            parsed_responses.append(
138	                parsing_fn(raw_prompt + r, kwargs["function_name"][i])
139	            )
140	    else:
141	        parsed_responses = [parsing_fn(r) for r in responses]
142	
143	    correctness_rewards = process_answers_fn(parsed_responses, answer, pos_reward)
144	
145	    if alpha == 0.0:
146	        return correctness_rewards
147	    else:
148	        return [
149	            reward - alpha * steps / L
150	            for reward, steps in zip(correctness_rewards, n_steps)
151	        ]
152	
153	
154	def xml_mult_reward(*args, **kwargs) -> list[float]:
155	    """
156	    Combines strict XML-format numeric answer matching with step-based reward scaling.
157	    """
158	    return _multiplicative_step_scaling_reward_func(
159	        extract_gsm_answer, _process_answers_gsm8k, *args, **kwargs
160	    )
161	
162	
163	def xml_add_reward(*args, **kwargs) -> list[float]:
164	    """
165	    Combines strict XML-format numeric answer matching with step-based reward scaling.
166	    """
167	    return _additive_compute_reward_func(
168	        extract_gsm_answer, _process_answers_gsm8k, *args, **kwargs
169	    )

171	
172	def extract_answer_math(r) -> str | None:
173	    try:
174	        r = remove_boxed(last_boxed_only_string(r))
175	        return r
176	    except Exception:
177	        return None
178	
179	
180	def _process_answers_math(parsed_responses, answer, pos_reward) -> list[float]:
181	    answer = [remove_boxed(last_boxed_only_string(a)) for a in answer]
182	    return [
183	        pos_reward if is_equiv(r, a) else 0.0 for r, a in zip(parsed_responses, answer)
184	    ]

188	    *args,
189	    **kwargs,
190	) -> list[float]:
191	    r"""Extracts answers from the completions using `parsing_fn`, then computes a reward
192	    of the form $\delta(\hat{y} = y) \cdot \left(\frac{L - steps + 1}{L}\right)^\alpha.
193	    Uses float comparison of extracted numbers instead of exact string matching.
194	    """
195	    return _multiplicative_step_scaling_reward_func(
196	        extract_answer_math, _process_answers_math, *args, **kwargs
197	    )
198	
199	
200	def math_correctness_add_reward(
201	    *args,
202	    **kwargs,
203	) -> list[float]:
204	    return _additive_compute_reward_func(
205	        extract_answer_math, _process_answers_math, *args, **kwargs
206	    )

208	
209	def evaluate_code(generations, tests, pos_reward):
210	    """Evaluate code generations using HuggingFace's code_eval metric.
211	
212	    :param generations: List of generation dicts from evaluate()
213	    :param tests: List of test cases
214	    :param pos_reward: Positive reward multiplier
215	    :return: List of pass@1 results
216	    """
217	    pass_at_1s = []
218	    for gen, test in zip(generations, tests):
219	        try:
220	            # # Since we have 1 generation per task, wrap each in a list
221	            predictions = [[gen]]
222	            # references = [[test] for test in tests]
223	            references = [test]
224	
225	            # Compute pass@k with k=[1]
226	            # Returns tuple: (pass_at_k_dict, results_dict)
227	            pass_at_k, _ = code_eval.compute(
228	                references=references, predictions=predictions, k=[1]
229	            )
230	
231	            pass_at_1 = pass_at_k["pass@1"] * pos_reward
232	
233	            pass_at_1s.append(pass_at_1)
234	
235	        except Exception as e:
236	            print(f"Error during code evaluation: {e}")
237	            import traceback
238	
239	            traceback.print_exc()
240	            pass_at_1s.append(0.0)
241	
242	    return pass_at_1s
243

247	    **kwargs,

250	        sanitize_humaneval, evaluate_code, *args, **kwargs
251	    )

content.thinking:
Let me understand:

The user says the 2-way eval results were "not too bad." So the 2-way policy *does* produce reasonable outputs at eval time (like the baselines they ran earlier got ~50% on 4 GSM8K problems). But during *training*, reward=0 for all completions.

This suggests: the model CAN generate decent answers when using a heuristic baseline for unmasking. But during training, the *policy* is controlling unmasking and it's not doing it well enough.

Actually wait - the 2-way run is the training run for the 2-way policy (`policy2way`), and it also showed reward=0. 

So the real issue is becoming clearer: heuristic baselines work fine at eval time, but when I train either the 2-way or 3-way policy, the RL loop never produces correct answers during training. This points to a problem with how the policy is learning to unmask, not with the 3-way approach itself. Now I'm looking at the reward function to see how it's evaluating answers on GSM8K problems.

The parsing logic routes differently depending on the dataset—GSM8K uses one extraction method while MATH uses another. But looking at the 2-way training stats, the policy is almost fully unmasking tokens (mean_unmask_prob of 0.124 suggests it's keeping most of the original text), yet the completions still aren't producing correct answers. The issue might be that even with nearly complete unmasking, the denoising process is generating garbage rather than valid solutions.

Comparing this to Jazbec et al.'s approach, their policy also starts conservative but uses a specific initialization that gradually unmaskes tokens over 32 steps. The difference might be in how the unmasking order affects coherence—maybe the timing and sequence of which tokens get revealed matters more than just the final unmasking percentage.

But if LLaDA-8B typically achieves 30-60%+ on GSM8K with reasonable unmasking, getting zero reward suggests something else is wrong. The training mixture might be the culprit—if most of the batch is MATH problems rather than GSM8K, the success rate would drop significantly. Or there could be a format mismatch where the model's completions don't align with what the answer extraction function expects.

The user mentioned eval on the 2-way looked decent though, so I should focus on whether the policy actually learned something despite the zero training reward signal. The core issue is clear: all three runs show zero reward and zero gradient norm, meaning the training loop isn't actually updating weights at all. The configs are similar enough that this points to a fundamental problem with how the training is set up, not a configuration mismatch. I need to ask what they mean by "eval on the 2way seemed not too bad" — whether they're looking at a heuristic baseline or an actual policy evaluation — but the real priority is understanding why learning has completely stalled across the board.

content.text:
Right — the heuristic baseline evals work fine because they bypass the policy entirely and just use LLaDA-8B with simple heuristics (top-K, etc.). The model itself generates decent answers. The problem is specifically during **GRPO training**: the policy-controlled generation never produces a single correct answer.

## The real issue across all 3 runs

This is **not** a 2-way vs 3-way or 4090 vs A100 issue. All three training runs share:

| Metric | 2-way (londine) | 3-way (you, A100) | 3-way (Ryan, 4090) |
|---|---|---|---|
| reward | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| zero_std_ratio | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| loss | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| grad_norm | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| still_masked | 0.003 | 0.004 | 0.004 |

Tokens ARE getting unmasked (still_masked ≈ 0). Full-length completions ARE being generated (completion_length ≈ 239). But the reward parser **never** finds a correct answer. Across ~128,000+ completions total. That's not bad luck — something is systematically broken.

## Most likely diagnosis: the generations are garbled

You saw this yourself in the local smoke test — even at eval time with a heuristic baseline, the outputs had lots of repetition (`of of of of`, `half half half`). During training with a *policy* controlling unmasking (especially an untrained one), the generation quality is even worse. The answer parser (`extract_gsm_answer`) looks for `<answer>\boxed{...}</answer>` format. If the generation is garbled enough that this pattern never appears, every completion scores 0.

**This is the cold-start problem.** The initial policy is so conservative/random that the unmasking order produces gibberish → reward = 0 → no gradient → policy never improves → still gibberish. A death spiral.

## What to investigate

Can you pull a sample completion from the wandb logs? On one of the running training jobs:

```bash
# check if the trainer logs sample completions
grep -r "wandb_log_unique_prompts\|log_completions\|sample_output" train/trainer.py | head -20
```

Or check the wandb **Logs** tab for that run — if the trainer prints sample generations, look at what the model is actually producing during training. If it's `half half half half \boxed{3}` that's one problem; if it's `[MASK][MASK][MASK]` or pure garbage with no `\boxed{}` at all, that's the cold-start issue.

**The fix depends on what the generations look like.** Want me to dig into `train/trainer.py` to find where completions are generated and how the policy interacts with the generation loop during training?

content.text:
Here's what they mean:

| Metric | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `remask_rate/early` | 0 | Fraction of active positions remasked during denoising steps 0 – T/3. **Zero — no remasking in the early phase.** |
| `remask_rate/mid` | 0.00002 | Fraction remasked during steps T/3 – 2T/3. Essentially zero. |
| `remask_rate/late` | 0.00002 | Fraction remasked during steps 2T/3 – T. Essentially zero. |
| `remask_conf_mean` | 0.998 | When a token *does* get remasked, its confidence was ~99.8%. The policy is remasking **high-confidence tokens** — the ones it should be leaving alone. |

## What this tells you

The policy is **almost never remasking** (rate ≈ 0.002% of positions). And the rare times it does, it's remasking tokens the base model is 99.8% confident about — exactly the wrong ones. It should be remasking *low*-confidence tokens (the ones that were likely mistakes).

But this is a symptom, not the cause. The cause is still the same: `reward=0`, `grad_norm=0` → the policy has never received a gradient update. These remask rates are just the random noise from the initial weights. The policy hasn't *learned* to remask wrong tokens because it's never learned anything at all.

**The fix is still upstream of these metrics** — you need to solve the cold-start problem (reward=0 death spiral) first. Once the policy starts getting *some* non-zero reward signal, these remask rates should start moving in a meaningful direction.

Have you been able to check the actual generated text from any of the training runs? That's the fastest way to diagnose whether the generations are complete garbage vs. reasonable-but-unparseable. Check the wandb **Logs** tab or look for a `wandb_log_unique_prompts` flag.

content.text:
`train/still_masked` = fraction of tokens in the completion that are **still `[MASK]` at the end of generation**, averaged across the batch.

```
still_masked = (still_masked.float() / gen_length).mean()
```

Your value: **0.004** = 0.4% of tokens still masked after all denoising steps finish.

That's actually fine — it means **99.6% of tokens get unmasked by the end**. The generation loop is completing, the policy is eventually unmasking nearly everything. The problem isn't that tokens stay masked — it's that the *order* and *timing* of unmasking produces incoherent text that the reward parser can't extract a correct answer from.

content.text:
You're on the A100 instance, not the L4. Let me answer all three:

**1. View training output — no conda needed, just read the logs:**

```bash
# See the most recent training log (the v2 run that's on wandb)
tail -100 train3way_v2.log

# Or search for sample completions/rewards in the log
grep -i "reward\|boxed\|answer\|generation" train3way_v2.log | tail -50
```

**2. Check if training is still running:**

```bash
nvidia-smi
```

If GPU shows a python process using ~30+ GB → still training. If GPU is idle → it stopped or crashed.

Also check:
```bash
ps aux | grep train.train
```

**3. Check the auto-restart script:**

```bash
cat auto_restart_training.sh
```

This will show you what it does. To see when it last ran:

```bash
# Check if it's running as a background process
ps aux | grep auto_restart

# Check system logs for recent runs
ls -la train3way*.log

# See the start/end of each log
head -5 train3way.log
head -5 train3way_v2.log
tail -5 train3way_v2.log
```

Paste the output of these and I'll tell you exactly what's happening — whether the run is live, whether it auto-restarted, and what the model is actually generating.

content.text:
Wait — this log is actually **much better** than the wandb summary suggested. I see **signs of life** in the recent steps:

## Non-zero rewards ARE happening

| Step | Reward | zero_std_ratio | grad_norm | What happened |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1037 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.054 | 1/16 prompts got a correct answer |
| 1039 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.019 | ✓ gradient update |
| 1085 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.006 | ✓ gradient update |
| 1091 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.021 | ✓ gradient update |
| 1095 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.039 | ✓ gradient update |
| **1111** | **0.125** | **0.5** | **0.049** | **2/16 prompts correct — best so far** |
| 1113 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.013 | ✓ gradient update |
| 1119 | 0.0625 | 0.5 | 0.016 | ✓ gradient update |

That's ~8 non-zero reward steps out of the last ~50 logged steps (~16% hit rate). Not great, but **not dead** either.

## Positive trends in the last ~100 steps

| Metric | Step ~1031 | Step ~1129 | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| `entropy` | 0.057 | **0.093** | ↑ Rising — policy is exploring more |
| `action_rate/unmask` | 0.012 | **0.019** | ↑ Rising — unmasking more aggressively |
| `action_rate/keep` | 0.988 | **0.981** | ↓ Loosening its grip on "keep" |

The wandb summary at step 1098 just happened to land on a zero-reward step, which made it look completely dead. But the trend over the last 100 steps shows the policy **is slowly learning to unmask more**, and it's hitting correct answers more frequently as a result.

## The honest assessment

This is the cold-start thawing — very slowly. The ~16% hit rate means GRPO gets a useful gradient signal ~1 in 6 steps, and the rest are wasted. Compare to what Jazbec et al. probably saw with 2-way: a much higher initial hit rate because the 2-way action space is simpler (sigmoid unmask probability is easier to explore than a 3-way softmax).

**Let it keep running.** The entropy and unmask rate are trending in the right direction. If this continues, you should see reward climbing over the next few hundred steps as the policy learns to unmask more aggressively → more readable completions → more correct answers → stronger gradient signal → virtuous cycle.

Check back in a few hours and look for:
- `entropy` above 0.15 (currently 0.09)
- `reward` consistently above 0.1
- `unmask rate` above 0.03

If those three are trending up, training is working and just needs more time. If they plateau or drop, then you'll need to intervene (raise `policy_smart_init`, add entropy bonus, etc.).

| Non-zero reward | ~1 in 6 steps | Thawing, not dead |

## The time problem

At 55 sec/step, finishing all 14,973 steps takes **~9.5 more days of continuous A100 time**. If this is a Spot A100, it'll get preempted multiple times (but `resume_from_checkpoint: true` + `save_steps: 50` means you lose at most ~45 min per preemption).

**Cost:** if this is GCP A100 at $1.10/hr (spot): ~$250 for the full run. If on-demand ($3.67/hr): ~$830. Check which you're using — this matters.

## What to do right now

**Don't kill it.** The trends are positive. Let it run and check back in 6–12 hours. Look for:

```bash
tail -20 train3way_v2.log | grep reward
```

If `reward` is consistently above 0.1 and `entropy` is above 0.15, the cold start is breaking and you're on track. If reward is still mostly 0 after another 500 steps, then the thaw is too slow and you'd want to consider restarting with a warmer `policy_smart_init` (like -1.0 or -0.5 instead of -2.0) to accelerate it.
so you're saying it used to get 0 things correct, but now it's getting 2/16 things correct?

Yes, exactly. That's what the log shows over the last ~100 steps:

- **Steps 1031–1035**: reward=0 every step. Zero correct out of 16 prompts. No gradient. Policy frozen.
- **Steps 1037–1129**: reward=0.0625 (~1/16 correct) popping up every ~10 steps, with occasional reward=0.125 (**2/16 correct** at step 1111). Real gradients flowing when this happens.

So it went from "never gets anything right" to "stumbles onto a correct answer ~16% of the time." That's not a lot, but it's the difference between **zero gradient signal** and **some gradient signal**, which is everything in RL.

The virtuous cycle you're hoping for: more unmasking → more readable text → more correct answers → stronger gradient → policy learns to unmask even better → repeat. The entropy and unmask rate trending up suggest this cycle is starting to kick in, just very slowly.

The question is whether it accelerates from here or stays at ~1/16 for thousands of steps. Check back in 6–12 hours — if reward has climbed to 0.2+ and entropy is above 0.15, you're in good shape. If it's still at 0.0625, you may want to restart with a warmer init.

|---|---|---|---|---|

**Essentially flat at ~0.001% of positions.** The policy is almost never choosing remask. Compare to unmask which went from 1.2% → 1.9% over the same window — that's real movement. Remask hasn't moved at all.

## Why this makes sense (and why it's not a problem yet)

The policy is still in cold-start recovery. Its current priority — correctly learned — is:

1. **First: learn to unmask enough to produce readable text** (happening now — unmask rate rising)
2. **Then: learn when to remask mistakes** (can't happen until the policy is unmasking enough to *make* mistakes worth correcting)

Remasking is a second-order skill. You can't learn "fix errors" if you're not generating enough text to have errors worth fixing. Right now the policy barely unmasks 2% of positions per step — there's almost nothing to remask.

**Expect remask to stay near zero until unmask rate climbs above ~5–10%** and the policy is regularly producing complete-ish answers (reward consistently > 0.2). At that point, some of those answers will be *wrong*, and the remasking action becomes the way to correct them. That's when you should see remask rates start climbing.

This is also exactly the analysis for Option B in the paper — "when does remasking fire?" If it only fires after the policy is already decent at unmasking, that tells you something about the learning dynamics. Track it.
